title: Right-Size Every Call: An AI Cost and Latency Router
one_line: Build middleware that routes each request between small and large models by predicted complexity, measuring the tradeoff across cost, latency, and quality.
why_it_matters: Sending every request to the largest model is the most common source of avoidable AI spend, but routing naively to a smaller model drops quality on the requests that needed the large one. The value is in a router that protects a quality floor while cutting cost and latency, and proves the tradeoff with numbers.
persona: Platform engineer running an LLM feature at scale
current_workflow: Every request goes to one large model regardless of difficulty, and cost and latency are reviewed monthly after the bill arrives, with no per-request complexity signal.
ai_workflow: The middleware scores each request for predicted complexity, routes simple requests to a small model and hard ones to a large model, and falls back to the large model when a small-model output fails a quality check. It logs cost, latency, and a quality score per request and reports the aggregate tradeoff against an all-large baseline.
inputs: a request stream with mixed difficulty
outputs: an aggregate tradeoff report against the all-large baseline
definition_of_done: On a synthetic request stream with labeled easy and hard items, the router cuts cost and latency against the all-large baseline while holding aggregate quality at or above the configured floor, and escalates failed small-model outputs to the large model rather than shipping them.
example_input: A batch of 1000 requests, 70 percent simple lookups and 30 percent multi-step reasoning, with an all-large baseline cost and quality recorded.
example_output: Report: cost down 58 percent, p95 latency down 40 percent, aggregate quality within 1 point of baseline, 4 percent of small-model outputs escalated to large on a failed quality check.
non_goals: training a router model from scratch
evaluation_ideas: cost and latency reduction against baseline
